subtopic_detail: Intrapartum GBS prophylaxis at preterm with unknown maternal carriage
stem:
You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 30-year-old G2P1 woman at 33+0 weeks gestation in spontaneous preterm labour. SROM occurred 2 hours ago with clear liquor. Contractions are 3 in 10 minutes, cervix is 4 cm dilated and 80 percent effaced. The 28-week swab was not done as planned and her 36-week LVS for GBS has not yet been collected.

Foetal heart rate is reassuring on CTG. Foetal lung maturity has not been induced. There is no maternal fever, no offensive discharge, no allergies and no antibiotic exposure in the previous 4 weeks. Tocolysis with nifedipine is being commenced to allow steroids and magnesium for neuroprotection.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal T 36.8, HR 92, BP 118/72; CTG: baseline 140 with normal variability, accelerations present, no decelerations
data_table.Examination: Cervix 4 cm dilated 80% effaced, vertex at -2 station, intact membranes ruptured 2 hours ago (clear liquor), no clinical chorioamnionitis
data_table.Investigations: FBC, CRP normal; bHCG previously confirmed; GBS swab not collected; no penicillin allergy; not previously colonised in last pregnancy

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic plan?

options.text: Withhold antibiotics until the result of an urgent GBS swab and avoid empirical treatment to limit unnecessary intrapartum antibiotic exposure and the risk of antimicrobial resistance and altered neonatal microbiome
options.rationale: At less than 37 weeks gestation with unknown GBS status, intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis should be commenced empirically without waiting for swab results, as labour onset before 37 weeks is itself an indication. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Commence intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose followed by 1.8 g every 4 hours until birth as intrapartum group B streptococcus prophylaxis given preterm labour and unknown maternal GBS carriage status
options.rationale: Preterm labour with unknown maternal GBS status is an indication for empirical intrapartum penicillin prophylaxis throughout labour to reduce neonatal early-onset GBS disease. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Commence intravenous erythromycin 250 mg every 6 hours as antibiotic of choice for preterm prelabour rupture of membranes only, which doubles as group B streptococcus prophylaxis throughout the duration of labour in this presentation
options.rationale: Erythromycin is the antibiotic of choice for PPROM not in labour to prolong latency; it is not adequate intrapartum GBS prophylaxis. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Commence intravenous cefazolin 2 g loading dose then 1 g every 8 hours as first-line group B streptococcus prophylaxis throughout labour because it provides equivalent neonatal protection with broader spectrum than penicillin alone
options.rationale: Cefazolin is reserved for mild non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy; penicillin is the first-line agent because of its narrow spectrum and excellent neonatal outcomes. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Group B Streptococcus)

options.text: Commence oral amoxicillin 500 mg every 8 hours during labour as adequate intrapartum group B streptococcus prophylaxis for the duration of established labour up to the birth of the baby in normal vaginal birth
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics do not achieve adequate intrapartum concentrations; intravenous benzylpenicillin is required at least 4 hours before birth for optimal neonatal protection. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Group B Streptococcus)

explanation.summary: Indications for intrapartum GBS prophylaxis in Australia include GBS bacteriuria in current pregnancy, positive GBS screening swab at 35-37 weeks, previous baby with invasive GBS disease, preterm labour less than 37 weeks with unknown GBS status, prolonged ROM at least 18 hours, and maternal intrapartum fever. First-line is IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g every 4 hours until birth. Cefazolin is for mild penicillin allergy; clindamycin (if sensitive) or vancomycin for anaphylactic allergy.
key_points: Preterm labour with unknown GBS status = treat empirically
First-line: IV benzylpenicillin 3 g load then 1.8 g 4-hourly
Goal: at least 4 hours of antibiotic before birth
explanation.pearls: Erythromycin for PPROM (ORACLE study) is to prolong latency, not for GBS prophylaxis; both interventions may be needed concurrently when preterm rupture progresses to labour.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Maternal GBS prophylaxis

subtopic_detail: Repeat IM adrenaline at adult dose for school-age child with refractory anaphylaxis
stem:
You are the paediatric emergency registrar reviewing a 6-year-old boy with known peanut allergy who accidentally ate a peanut butter biscuit at a party 10 minutes ago. He has developed urticaria, lip swelling, vomiting once, a hoarse voice, audible expiratory wheeze, and a feeling of throat tightness. His parent administered his prescribed EpiPen Jr (150 mcg adrenaline) into the lateral thigh 5 minutes ago at home with no clear improvement before transfer.

On arrival in the resuscitation cubicle he remains distressed, tachypnoeic and his oxygen saturation has fallen on room air. Weight at his last GP visit was 24 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9, HR 140, RR 36, BP 92/56, SpO2 92% room air
data_table.Examination: Diffuse urticaria, lip and tongue swelling, hoarse voice, audible expiratory wheeze, mild intercostal recession, alert but distressed, weight 24 kg
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 5.6, ECG sinus tachycardia, no IV access yet established, allergy passport documents peanut as known allergen, prior adrenaline auto-injector at home

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Repeat the EpiPen Jr 150 mcg IM into the opposite lateral thigh, reassess in 5 minutes and consider escalation only if no response, while preparing intravenous access and high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask in the resuscitation bay
options.rationale: At 6 years of age and 24 kg, the appropriate adrenaline dose has moved from the 150 mcg auto-injector to 300 mcg IM (1:1000 0.3 mL); repeating the junior dose underdoses. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis

options.text: Administer 0.3 mL of 1:1000 adrenaline (300 mcg) IM into the mid-anterolateral thigh, apply high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask, position the patient supine with legs elevated, and obtain IV access for fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: For a school-age child of 24 kg the standard IM adrenaline dose is 300 mcg (0.3 mL of 1:1000) into mid-anterolateral thigh, repeated every 5 minutes if needed; positioning and oxygen are concurrent. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis

options.text: Administer IV adrenaline 50 mcg bolus immediately under cardiac monitoring as the initial parenteral dose given the failure of the prehospital intramuscular dose to control the patient's airway and respiratory features
options.rationale: IV adrenaline is reserved for refractory anaphylaxis under continuous cardiac monitoring after multiple IM doses have failed; it is not the next step here. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis

options.text: Administer nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg via mask and IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg as primary therapy for the bronchospasm component, with adrenaline reserved as backup if there is no improvement over the next 10 minutes
options.rationale: Adrenaline is the first-line therapy in anaphylaxis; salbutamol and hydrocortisone are adjuncts but never primary, and delaying adrenaline worsens outcomes. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis

options.text: Administer oral promethazine 10 mg and ranitidine 75 mg as combined H1 and H2 antihistamine therapy with reassessment after 20 minutes, plus consideration of a single dose of IM prednisolone 1 mg/kg as additional steroid
options.rationale: Antihistamines and steroids do not treat airway compromise or shock in anaphylaxis; adrenaline is the only first-line drug. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis

explanation.summary: Anaphylaxis is treated with IM adrenaline 1:1000 into the mid-anterolateral thigh as first-line therapy; doses are 0.1 mL (10 kg or less), 0.15 mL (10-20 kg, EpiPen Jr), 0.3 mL (20-50 kg, EpiPen) and 0.5 mL (over 50 kg). Repeat every 5 minutes if symptoms persist. Concurrent oxygen, supine positioning with legs elevated, and IV fluid resuscitation are essential. Salbutamol, antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjuncts only.
key_points: First-line: IM adrenaline 1:1000 mid-anterolateral thigh, repeat every 5 min
Doses by weight: 10 kg = 0.1 mL; 20 kg = 0.15 mL; over 20 kg = 0.3 mL; over 50 kg = 0.5 mL
Adjuncts (steroid, antihistamine, salbutamol) never replace adrenaline
explanation.pearls: Refractory anaphylaxis after 2-3 IM doses warrants IV adrenaline infusion under cardiac monitoring with anaesthetic/ED senior input; biphasic reactions occur in up to 20% and warrant observation.

sources.label: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis

subtopic_detail: Hypertensive emergency with target end-organ damage and gradual BP reduction
stem:
You are the medical registrar reviewing a 58-year-old man in the emergency department with a severe occipital headache for 2 hours, blurred vision and one episode of vomiting. His BP is 220/130 in both arms with a regular pulse. Fundoscopy demonstrates bilateral flame-shaped haemorrhages and papilloedema. Creatinine has risen from baseline 90 to 180 with new microscopic haematuria and 2+ proteinuria.

He has no chest pain, focal neurological deficit, or pulmonary oedema, and the GCS is 15. ECG shows sinus rhythm with LVH by voltage criteria. He has not taken his usual amlodipine or perindopril for one week.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 220/130 both arms, HR 88, RR 18, SpO2 98% room air, GCS 15, T 36.8
data_table.Examination: Bilateral flame haemorrhages and papilloedema, no focal neurology, no carotid bruit, no chest signs, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Cr 180 (baseline 90), urine microscopic haematuria with 2+ proteinuria, ECG sinus rhythm with LVH voltage criteria, CXR no pulmonary oedema, troponin within normal range

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Admit to HDU and commence intravenous labetalol infusion titrated to reduce mean arterial pressure by about 25 percent in the first hour, then to BP around 160/100 over the next 6 hours, while investigating for end-organ damage
options.rationale: Hypertensive emergency requires gradual MAP reduction of around 25 percent in the first hour with a parenteral agent (typically labetalol or GTN) to avoid cerebral or renal hypoperfusion. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Cardiovascular: Hypertensive emergencies)

options.text: Commence IV sodium nitroprusside infusion targeting normalisation of BP to 120/80 within the first hour to minimise the risk of further hypertensive end-organ injury, with arterial line monitoring and cyanide level checking after 24 hours
options.rationale: Rapid normalisation risks cerebral and renal hypoperfusion; the standard target is a gradual ~25% MAP reduction in the first hour, not normalisation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Cardiovascular: Hypertensive emergencies)

options.text: Restart oral amlodipine 10 mg and perindopril 8 mg in the emergency department, allow an oral observation period of 4 to 6 hours and discharge home with general practitioner review in 24 hours for ongoing medication compliance
options.rationale: End-organ damage (papilloedema, AKI) defines hypertensive emergency rather than urgency, requiring IV therapy and admission, not outpatient oral restart. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Cardiovascular: Hypertensive emergencies)

options.text: Administer sublingual nifedipine 10 mg now and again in 30 minutes if BP remains elevated above 180/110 with bedside continuous monitoring, then transition to oral amlodipine therapy for ongoing maintenance once stable
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine causes uncontrolled BP drops with rebound and has been associated with stroke and MI; it is contraindicated in hypertensive emergency. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Cardiovascular: Hypertensive emergencies)

options.text: Administer GTN spray 0.4 mg sublingually as needed for symptom control, with repeat as required, alongside oral metoprolol 25 mg as monotherapy in the emergency department before bed-based ward admission for further blood pressure observation
options.rationale: Symptomatic GTN spray is insufficient for true hypertensive emergency with end-organ damage; IV infusion with titration is required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Cardiovascular: Hypertensive emergencies)

explanation.summary: Hypertensive emergency is defined by acute or worsening end-organ damage (retinopathy with haemorrhages or papilloedema, AKI, encephalopathy, pulmonary oedema, aortic dissection, eclampsia) rather than absolute BP value. Management is admission to HDU/ICU with IV antihypertensive (commonly labetalol, GTN, or nicardipine) titrated to reduce MAP by about 25 percent in the first hour, then to about 160/100 over 6 hours, then normalisation over 24-48 hours. Rapid normalisation causes cerebral hypoperfusion.
key_points: Hypertensive emergency = end-organ damage, regardless of absolute BP
Reduce MAP by 25% in first hour, not faster
Sublingual nifedipine and rapid normalisation are unsafe
explanation.pearls: Exceptions to the 25% rule: aortic dissection (SBP <120 in 20 min), ischaemic stroke (only treat above 220/120 unless thrombolysis), and pre-eclampsia (treat above 160/110 promptly).

subtopic_detail: Postpartum depression with active suicidal ideation requiring inpatient mother-baby admission
stem:
You are the GP reviewing a 28-year-old woman 8 weeks postpartum after her first baby, a healthy term boy now exclusively breastfed. She presents with low mood, anhedonia, marked guilt about being 'a bad mother', insomnia beyond infant sleep disruption, and weight loss. EPDS score today is 22 with item 10 endorsed. On further interview she describes recurrent suicidal thoughts with a tentative plan involving medications at home.

She denies any intent or thoughts to harm the baby. Her husband works long hours and there is no extended family nearby. She has no prior psychiatric history and is taking no medications.

data_table.Examination: Flat affect, tearful, slowed speech, denies psychotic symptoms, denies thoughts to harm baby, infant settled and well-cared-for at this visit
data_table.Investigations: EPDS 22 with positive item 10, TSH 1.8 (normal), Hb 122, ferritin 38, urine drug screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Commence oral fluoxetine 20 mg daily, arrange community perinatal mental health team review tomorrow and ensure husband is aware to provide support, with discharge home today and follow-up safety planning by phone in 48 hours
options.rationale: Active suicidal ideation with plan in the context of severe postpartum depression and limited social support warrants admission, not community follow-up; fluoxetine also has a long half-life and is not first-line during breastfeeding. (Source: COPE Perinatal Mental Health Guidelines)

options.text: Arrange same-day admission to a mother-baby unit for combined inpatient assessment and treatment, commence sertraline as the SSRI most compatible with breastfeeding, and involve the perinatal psychiatry team for ongoing management
options.rationale: Severe postpartum depression with active suicidal ideation and plan requires inpatient care; mother-baby units in Australia allow continued breastfeeding and bonding while ensuring safety. (Source: COPE Perinatal Mental Health Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for outpatient cognitive behavioural therapy through the Better Access program with focus on cognitive restructuring and behavioural activation, with weekly EPDS reassessment by GP for the next 6 weeks before considering medication or admission
options.rationale: Psychological therapy alone is inadequate for severe postpartum depression with active suicidal ideation; safety must be addressed first via admission and pharmacotherapy. (Source: COPE Perinatal Mental Health Guidelines)

options.text: Admit to general adult psychiatry inpatient unit with separation of mother and baby for the duration of admission, commence sertraline 50 mg daily, and arrange community follow-up with paediatric review for the infant after discharge
options.rationale: Separation of mother and baby is harmful to bonding and breastfeeding; mother-baby units are preferred where available in Australia. (Source: COPE Perinatal Mental Health Guidelines)

options.text: Commence diazepam 5 mg twice daily for anxiety and insomnia, with brief sleep advice, and reassess in 1 week with view to commencing antidepressant therapy once sleep has been restored and mood appears more stable on benzodiazepine cover
options.rationale: Diazepam is not first-line for postpartum depression and crosses into breastmilk significantly; it does not treat the underlying condition and delays definitive care. (Source: COPE Perinatal Mental Health Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Postpartum depression with active suicidal ideation and a plan, particularly with limited social support, is a psychiatric emergency requiring admission to a mother-baby unit where available. Sertraline is the SSRI of choice during breastfeeding due to minimal infant exposure. Fluoxetine is avoided because of its long half-life and active metabolite. Psychological therapy and community follow-up have a role only when safety is established.
key_points: EPDS >=13 plus active suicidal ideation = high risk; consider admission
Mother-baby units preserve bonding and breastfeeding
Sertraline preferred SSRI in breastfeeding; avoid fluoxetine (long half-life)
explanation.pearls: Item 10 of EPDS (thoughts of self-harm) endorsement always warrants direct safety assessment and explicit suicide risk evaluation, regardless of total score.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines: Perinatal Mental Health

subtopic_detail: Hyperemesis gravidarum with dehydration and electrolyte disturbance requiring admission
stem:
You are the obstetric registrar in the early pregnancy assessment unit reviewing a 26-year-old G1P0 woman at 9 weeks gestation by certain LMP with confirmed intrauterine singleton on prior scan. She has had persistent vomiting more than 10 times daily for 4 days, is unable to tolerate even sips of water, has lost 4.5 kg (6 percent of pre-pregnancy weight), and feels light-headed on standing.

She has tried oral ondansetron wafers and dietary modification at home without effect. Urine on arrival shows ketones ++++. There is no fever, abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding or thyroid symptoms, and her TSH on screening was 1.4.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 105/65 lying, 88/52 standing with HR 122, T 36.7, RR 18, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 3 sec, soft abdomen with no peritonism, no goitre, no thyroid signs
data_table.Investigations: Na 131, K 3.2, Cl 86, HCO3 28, urea 8.4, Cr 88, ALT 42, urine ketones ++++, beta-hCG appropriate for gestation, USS confirms viable IUP

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Admit for IV rehydration with Hartmann solution, IV ondansetron 4 mg 8-hourly, IV thiamine 100 mg before any glucose-containing fluid, potassium replacement and recheck electrolytes in 6 hours with daily ketone monitoring
options.rationale: Severe hyperemesis with hypokalaemia, ketonuria and orthostatic hypotension requires admission for IV rehydration, parenteral antiemetic, IV thiamine before glucose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy, and electrolyte correction. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Hyperemesis Gravidarum)

options.text: Discharge home with oral metoclopramide 10 mg 8-hourly and oral pyridoxine 25 mg 8-hourly with advice to sip clear fluids and review GP in 24 hours, with safety net advice to return if symptoms or weight loss progress
options.rationale: Orthostatic hypotension, hypokalaemia 3.2 and 6 percent weight loss are objective markers of severe hyperemesis warranting admission rather than community oral therapy. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Hyperemesis Gravidarum)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 40 mg daily for 1 week then taper as first-line therapy for refractory hyperemesis given her failed home antiemetic therapy, with admission only if symptoms have not resolved after the steroid course
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are reserved for severe refractory hyperemesis after first- and second-line antiemetics plus IV rehydration have failed in the inpatient setting. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Hyperemesis Gravidarum)

options.text: Admit for IV haloperidol 1 mg 8-hourly as third-line antiemetic monotherapy because of failure of first-line oral therapies, with no role for hydration as the patient is otherwise stable apart from intermittent vomiting symptoms
options.rationale: Haloperidol is a later-line antiemetic and is not used in isolation without rehydration; the patient is clearly dehydrated and needs IV fluids. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Hyperemesis Gravidarum)

options.text: Commence total parenteral nutrition via central line within the first 24 hours of admission to provide complete nutritional support, with concurrent ondansetron 8 mg every 8 hours and weight gain targets reviewed daily by dietetics
options.rationale: TPN is reserved for prolonged severe cases with significant nutritional deficit unresponsive to enteral support; it is not first-line at this stage and has significant complication rates. (Source: RANZCOG Statement: Hyperemesis Gravidarum)

explanation.summary: Hyperemesis gravidarum is diagnosed by intractable nausea and vomiting in pregnancy with weight loss (typically >5%), dehydration and electrolyte disturbance. Admission criteria include inability to tolerate oral fluids, ketonuria, weight loss, electrolyte derangement, or failed outpatient management. Inpatient care includes IV rehydration with crystalloid, electrolyte correction (especially potassium), IV thiamine 100 mg before any dextrose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy, and parenteral antiemetic (ondansetron, metoclopramide, or promethazine).
key_points: Admission triggers: weight loss >5%, ketonuria, hypokalaemia, orthostatic hypotension
Always thiamine before glucose-containing fluids (Wernicke prevention)
Steroids and TPN are last-line for refractory cases
explanation.pearls: Ondansetron has reassuring safety data in pregnancy; the historical first-trimester cleft palate signal has not been confirmed in larger Australian and international studies.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline 69: Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy
